His local legacy was disaster. His attempt to impose imperial bailiffs — the “Lombards” — on the kingdom on his departure provoked the War of the Lombards (1229–1243), a fifteen-year civil war won in Acre and Cyprus by the Ibelin-led baronial party and producing the most fully articulated feudal constitution of the Middle Ages, the Assises de Jérusalem. Frederick never returned. His son Conrad II succeeded on paper; the kingdom was run from the High Court.

entries.name: Conrad II
entries.short: King of Jerusalem (1228–1254)
body: Son of Frederick II and Isabella II, Conrad — known in German history as Conrad IV, in Jerusalem as Conrad II — became King of Jerusalem at eight days old, on his mother's death in childbirth in May 1228. He never set foot in Outremer. The kingdom was run in his name by a succession of regents and, after 1243, by the barons of the High Court themselves.

In 1243, on the grounds that the king had come of age but had failed to appear in person to receive the homage of his vassals, the High Court formally ended the regency of Frederick's representatives and elected Alice of Champagne — the long-exiled senior dowager — as regent in her own right. The kingdom was from that moment a feudal republic in all but name. The following year, in the weakest hour of this arrangement, the Khwarazmian horsemen hired by the Egyptian sultan as-Salih Ayyub sacked Jerusalem for good; the Holy City was never again in Frankish hands.

Conrad spent the 1240s and early 1250s fighting to hold his father's shattered inheritance in Germany and Italy. He died of fever at Lavello in southern Italy in May 1254, aged twenty-six, leaving a two-year-old son — Conradin — and a kingdom that, by now, he had never seen and never would.

entries.name: Conradin
entries.short: King of Jerusalem (1254–1268)
body: Conradin — Conrad the Younger, last of the Hohenstaufen — was two years old when his father's death made him King of Jerusalem. He lived his short life in Italy and Bavaria and, like his father before him, never came east. The kingdom in his name was governed entirely through the baronial High Court and a series of regents drawn from the Lusignan kings of Cyprus.

This absence produced the one thing Outremer did best in the thirteenth century: law. Deprived of a resident king, jurists like Philip of Novara, John of Ibelin of Jaffa and James of Ibelin spent decades codifying the customs of the High Court into the most detailed feudal constitution of the Middle Ages — the Assises de Jérusalem — which would still be quoted by Venetian lawyers in the sixteenth century. The kingdom's territory continued to shrink under the Mamluk sultans Baybars and Qalawun, but its legal culture flourished.

Conradin's end was operatic. In 1268, sixteen years old, he led a German expedition into southern Italy to recover his Hohenstaufen patrimony from Charles of Anjou. Charles defeated him at Tagliacozzo, captured him days later, and beheaded him in the main square of Naples on 29 October 1268. With his execution the Hohenstaufen line ended, and the Jerusalem throne passed, by the ruling of the High Court, to his cousin Hugh III of Cyprus.

entries.name: Hugh I
entries.short: King of Jerusalem & Cyprus (1268–1284)
body: Already King of Cyprus since 1267 and grandson of Hugh I of Cyprus through the female line, Hugh of the Poitiers-Lusignan house was chosen by the High Court of Acre on Conradin's execution in 1268, against the rival claim of his cousin Hugh of Brienne. He was the first resident King of Jerusalem in forty years — a genuine attempt to reassemble a functioning monarchy in the kingdom's final hour. In Jerusalem he reigned as Hugh I.

He spent the next sixteen years trying to rule two kingdoms and two sets of vassals separated by a hundred miles of sea. His Cypriot knights routinely refused to serve on the mainland, on the grounds that their feudal obligation was to defend the island. His mainland authority was undermined from 1277, when Maria of Antioch sold her parallel dynastic claim to Charles of Anjou, the Angevin king of Sicily; Angevin troops seized Acre and compelled Hugh to retreat to Tyre and then Cyprus. His brief cooperation with Prince Edward of England during the Lord Edward's Crusade of 1271 managed a ten-year truce with Sultan Baybars but no permanent recovery.

Contemporaries called him handsome and charming and noted, with some understatement, that he had a temper. He was a patron of French romance literature, founded Cistercian houses in Cyprus, and was the dedicatee of Philip of Novara's political memoirs. Historians judge him an able ruler who arrived too late — the Templar–Hospitaller and Venetian–Genoese feuds inside the surviving kingdom made unity impossible, and the Mamluk Sultanate was by now overwhelmingly stronger than the coastal enclave he was trying to save.

entries.name: John II
entries.short: King of Jerusalem & Cyprus (1284–1285)
body: Eldest surviving son of Hugh I, John succeeded his father in both kingdoms in 1284. He was crowned in Tyre as John II of Jerusalem — his Cypriot numbering counts him as John I — and immediately faced the same structural problem his father had never solved: mainland barons who resented him, Cypriot vassals who resented being taxed for the mainland, and a Mamluk sultan (Qalawun) who was methodically picking off Frankish strongholds.

He survived the throne for less than a year. In May 1285 he died suddenly in Nicosia, aged about seventeen, amid the kind of poisoning rumours that attend any quick royal death in a faction-ridden court. No contemporary chronicler committed himself on the question. His younger brother Henry succeeded him.
His reign is a historical footnote — too brief for policy, too contested for legacy — but his death mattered, because Henry II inherited a kingdom down to its last six years on the mainland.

entries.name: Henry II
entries.short: King of Jerusalem & Cyprus (1285–1291)
body: Henry II was the last crowned King of Jerusalem to rule on the mainland. He succeeded his brother John II in 1285, forced the Angevin garrison out of Acre in 1286, and on 15 August of that year was crowned in the cathedral of Tyre — the last coronation a King of Jerusalem would celebrate on Levantine soil. He was fourteen. He suffered from epilepsy throughout his reign.

When the Mamluk sultan al-Ashraf Khalil moved against Acre in April 1291 with the largest siege army the Crusades had seen, Henry sailed from Cyprus with two hundred knights and a few thousand foot to join the defence. For six weeks, with the Templars, Hospitallers, Teutonic Knights and the remnants of his own household, he held the walls. When the Accursed Tower fell on 18 May and the city dissolved into street fighting, Henry was evacuated by galley to Cyprus — the act that, legally, ended the Kingdom of Jerusalem on the mainland.

From Cyprus he went on reigning as King of Jerusalem in title for thirty-three more years, petitioning Pope Clement V for a new crusade, attempting to impose a papal trade embargo on the Mamluks, and drafting careful plans for the reconquest that no one in Europe seriously intended to carry out. He died in Nicosia in 1324. He is the tragic figure of crusader historiography — the king who held the end.

entries.name: Balian of Ibelin
entries.short: The Ibelin lord who negotiated Jerusalem's surrender to Saladin in 1187 and became one of the defining baronial figures of the kingdom's crisis years.
body: Balian of Ibelin rose from the younger branch of a frontier lordly family into the centre of royal politics through his marriage to Maria Comnena, widow of King Amalric I and lady of Nablus. By the 1180s he stood among the leading opponents of Guy of Lusignan's court party, aligned with Raymond III of Tripoli and the older native baronage that feared a reckless confrontation with Saladin.

After Hattin, Balian entered Jerusalem to retrieve his family but was pressed by the inhabitants to take command of the city's defence. With no realistic hope of relief, he negotiated directly with Saladin and secured terms of ransom and evacuation that spared Jerusalem the indiscriminate slaughter that had marked its capture in 1099. His later career tied the Ibelins to the recovered coastal kingdom, and his descendants became the dominant legal and political dynasty of the thirteenth-century Latin East.

entries.name: Raymond III of Tripoli
entries.short: Count of Tripoli, prince of Galilee by marriage, and regent of Jerusalem; his rivalry with Guy of Lusignan shaped the kingdom's last years before Hattin.
body: Raymond III inherited the County of Tripoli as a child and spent much of his youth in Muslim captivity, an experience that helped make him one of the most cautious and diplomatically minded Frankish leaders of his generation. Through his marriage to Eschiva of Bures he also became Prince of Galilee, placing him at the centre of the northern frontier and the politics of Tiberias.

As regent for the child-king Baldwin V, Raymond tried to preserve a fragile balance between the kingdom's rival factions and Saladin's growing power. His opposition to Guy of Lusignan made him appear almost treasonous to his enemies, especially after he negotiated a local truce with Saladin in Galilee. Yet at Hattin he fought with the royal army and escaped the battlefield only as the kingdom collapsed around him. He died later that year, a figure remembered both as a prudent statesman and as a symbol of the fatal divisions of 1186–87.

entries.name: Bohemond I of Antioch
entries.short: Norman commander of the First Crusade and founder of the Principality of Antioch, the northernmost and most Byzantine-facing of the Crusader states.
body: Bohemond of Taranto was already a seasoned Norman warlord before he took the cross, having fought the Byzantine Empire in the Balkans under his father Robert Guiscard. During the First Crusade he became one of the expedition's most effective military leaders, especially at Antioch, where his contacts inside the city helped the crusaders seize it in June 1098.

Rather than continue immediately to Jerusalem, Bohemond kept Antioch for himself, founding a Norman principality in a city the Byzantines regarded as rightfully theirs. That decision shaped the politics of the northern Crusader states for generations: Antioch was at once a Latin frontier principality, an Armenian neighbour, a Syrian power, and a standing provocation to Constantinople. Bohemond's career gives the First Crusade its hard political edge, where pilgrimage and conquest became permanent lordship.

entries.name: Tancred
entries.short: Norman crusader commander who linked the conquest generation to the early rule of Galilee and Antioch.
body: Tancred, nephew of Bohemond of Taranto, was one of the most energetic commanders of the First Crusade. He fought across Anatolia and Syria, claimed Bethlehem shortly before the capture of Jerusalem, and after 1099 became one of the principal builders of Frankish authority in the interior of the new kingdom.

His first major lordship was Galilee, where he held Tiberias and helped anchor the exposed northern approaches of Jerusalem. He later governed Antioch as regent, defending the principality after Bohemond's capture and extending Norman influence in northern Syria. Tancred's career shows how fluid the first generation of Crusader rule remained: the same commander could be a royal vassal in Galilee, a prince-maker in Antioch, and a frontier warlord negotiating with Armenians, Byzantines, and Muslim emirs.

entries.name: Nur ad-Din
entries.short: Zengid ruler of Aleppo and Damascus whose unification of Muslim Syria prepared the way for Saladin's encirclement of the Crusader states.
body: Nur ad-Din inherited Aleppo after the assassination of his father Zengi and gradually turned the Zengid war effort from a regional dynasty into a sustained programme of jihad against the Franks. His capture of Damascus in 1154 transformed the strategic map: for the first time since the First Crusade, the Kingdom of Jerusalem faced a ruler who could coordinate Syria from Aleppo to the Hauran.

Although later overshadowed by Saladin, Nur ad-Din created much of the political and ideological framework Saladin inherited. He supported campaigns against Antioch and Edessa, pressed the Franks from the north and east, and sent Shirkuh and Saladin into Egypt when Amalric I tried to dominate the collapsing Fatimid caliphate. The union of Syria and Egypt that ultimately doomed the kingdom began under Nur ad-Din's pressure.

entries.name: Zengi
entries.short: Atabeg of Mosul and Aleppo whose capture of Edessa in 1144 shattered the first Crusader state and triggered the Second Crusade.
body: Zengi was a Turkish military ruler who built a powerful principality around Mosul and Aleppo at a time when Muslim Syria was still politically divided. His rise mattered because it gave the northern frontier a determined and mobile enemy, able to strike between the County of Edessa, Antioch, and the Jazira.

In 1144 Zengi captured Edessa, the first of the Crusader states founded after 1099. The fall shocked Latin Christendom and led directly to the preaching of the Second Crusade. Zengi was assassinated two years later, before he could exploit the victory fully, but his achievement changed the direction of the crusading movement: after Edessa, the Latin East was no longer expanding into a fragmented frontier but defending itself against increasingly coordinated Muslim powers.

entries.name: Baybars
entries.short: Mamluk sultan whose campaigns dismantled much of what remained of the Crusader states before the final fall of Acre.
body: Baybars rose from Kipchak slave-soldier origins to become one of the most formidable rulers of the medieval eastern Mediterranean. After the Mamluk victory over the Mongols at Ayn Jalut in 1260, he consolidated power in Egypt and Syria and turned his attention to the surviving Frankish enclaves along the coast.

His campaigns were systematic. Caesarea, Arsuf, Safed, Jaffa, Antioch, Montfort, and other fortresses fell or were destroyed under his pressure, while truces were used selectively to isolate one target from the next. Baybars did not end the Crusader states by himself, but he broke their strategic depth and made the final Mamluk conquest of Acre in 1291 possible.

entries.name: al-Kamil
entries.short: Ayyubid sultan of Egypt who faced the Fifth Crusade at Damietta and later negotiated Jerusalem's temporary restoration with Frederick II.
body: Al-Kamil, nephew of Saladin, ruled Egypt during one of the most diplomatically complex phases of the crusading era. During the Fifth Crusade he defended the Nile Delta against the crusader occupation of Damietta and repeatedly offered territorial concessions, including the return of Jerusalem, in exchange for evacuation. The crusade's leadership refused, advanced toward Cairo, and was trapped by the Nile flood before surrendering Damietta in 1221.

A decade later al-Kamil reached a very different settlement with Emperor Frederick II. The Treaty of Jaffa in 1229 returned Jerusalem, Bethlehem, and a corridor to the coast to Latin control, while leaving the Haram al-Sharif under Muslim authority. The agreement scandalised many contemporaries on both sides, but it restored the Holy City without a siege and shows how negotiation, dynastic rivalry, and military necessity could matter as much as battlefield victory.

entries.name: Italian Maritime Communes
entries.short: The Italian naval and merchant powers whose fleets, quarters, and privileges made the Crusader states commercially viable and politically unstable.
body: The Crusader states could not survive on cavalry alone. They needed ships: to bring pilgrims, settlers, horses, grain, siege engines, and money across the Mediterranean. Venice, Genoa, Pisa, and earlier Amalfi supplied that maritime lifeline, and in return received tax exemptions, courts, churches, warehouses, streets, and fortified quarters inside the great ports of the Latin East.

Their role was indispensable but disruptive. Venetian help made the capture of Tyre possible in 1124; Genoese and Pisan ships repeatedly shaped the fate of Acre, Jaffa, and other coastal cities. By the thirteenth century the rival communes inside Acre could behave almost like states within the state, and their commercial feuds helped turn the kingdom's capital into a factional battlefield. The Latin East was never only a feudal frontier; it was also a Mediterranean trading system.

entries.name: Turcopoles and Sergeants
entries.short: The non-knightly soldiers who filled out Crusader armies and garrisons, from local light cavalry to urban and castle sergeants.
body: Crusader warfare was never fought by knights alone. Sergeants served as infantry, garrison troops, crossbowmen, guards, and lesser mounted soldiers, while turcopoles were locally adapted light cavalry, often using eastern equipment and tactics better suited to scouting, raiding, and frontier warfare than the heavy charge of western knights.

The distinction mattered because the Latin East was chronically short of knights. Castles, cities, and lordships owed specified numbers of mounted knights, but they also depended on much larger bodies of sergeants to hold walls, guard roads, and sustain campaigns. The military orders employed turcopoles extensively, and their commanders, the turcopoliers, became important officers. Understanding them makes the kingdom's armies look less like a narrow aristocratic cavalry force and more like a mixed frontier military system.

entries.name: Latin Patriarchate of Jerusalem
entries.short: The Latin ecclesiastical hierarchy created after the conquest of Jerusalem, central to coronations, relics, diplomacy, and disputes over the Holy City.
body: After the capture of Jerusalem in 1099, the crusaders installed a Latin patriarch in place of the existing Greek Orthodox hierarchy and reorganised the sacred geography of the Holy Land under Latin ecclesiastical authority. The patriarchate controlled churches, estates, clergy, relic processions, and much of the ceremonial life that gave the kingdom its claim to be more than an ordinary frontier state.

The patriarch could crown kings, carry the True Cross in war, negotiate during crises, and place rulers or cities under ecclesiastical pressure. Relations with the monarchy were not always smooth: Daimbert of Pisa tried to make Jerusalem a church-ruled lordship under Godfrey and Baldwin I, while later patriarchs clashed with Frederick II after his excommunicated diplomacy restored Jerusalem in 1229. The patriarchate helps explain why politics in the kingdom so often passed through altars, relics, and coronations.

entries.name: True Cross
entries.short: The kingdom's most powerful battlefield relic, carried with the army as a sign of divine protection until its loss at Hattin.
body: The relic venerated as the True Cross was kept in Jerusalem and became the most potent sacred object of the Latin Kingdom. It was not merely devotional. When carried with the army, it signalled that the defence of the kingdom was a holy war fought under the protection of the Crucifixion itself, and its presence could stiffen morale in moments of extreme danger.

The relic accompanied Frankish armies at victories such as Montgisard and at the catastrophe of Hattin. Its capture by Saladin in 1187 was therefore a spiritual as well as military disaster: the army had been destroyed, the king's authority shattered, and the kingdom's central relic lost with it. Later crusaders repeatedly sought its return, but it never again functioned as the living war-standard of the Latin Kingdom.
